source.title: Hypocretin (orexin) neuromodulation of stress and reward pathways

confidence_rationale: Concise mechanistic review explicitly framing Hcrt as a neuromodulator that operates across both negative-valence (chronic stress, hyperarousal) and positive-valence (compulsive drug-seeking) dysregulated states. The bivalence is exactly the architectural commitment that distinguishes SD-037 from a simple threat-gating module: SD-037 commits to integrating drive+threat and outputting an override that can swing toward either threat-active coping or drive-active goal seeking. Source quality moderate-high (Curr Opin Neurobiol, de Lecea co-author). Mapping fidelity high at the architectural level. Confidence held at 0.74 because this is short-format review evidence rather than direct experimental measurement.
failure_signatures: If Hcrt acted only on threat or only on reward, SD-037's commitment that the override_signal integrates BOTH drive and threat context would be weakened. Giardino & de Lecea explicitly position Hcrt as bivalent across stress and reward pathways.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Hypocretin is a peptide neuromodulator expressed exclusively in the lateral hypothalamus that plays a fundamental role in wakefulness and arousal, and modulates dysregulated states of hyperarousal in both negative-valence (chronic stress) and positive-valence (compulsive drug-seeking) domains via projections to extended amygdala, brainstem, and mesolimbic pathways.
mapping.ree_translation: SD-037 commits to an override_signal computed from both drive_level (positive-valence input, SD-012) and sustained-threat context (negative-valence input, rolling z_harm window). The commitment to a single integrator that can output authority for either active coping or drive-driven goal seeking depends on biology that operates on both valence axes from one upstream source. Giardino & de Lecea provide that architectural cover at the review level.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The review describes Hcrt's role in dysregulated states (chronic stress, addiction) rather than in normal-range integration of drive and threat. SD-037's typical operating regime is the latter; the review's data are mostly from pathological extremes. Whether the same integrator-with-bivalent-output story holds in normal physiology is licensed by the wider Hcrt literature but not directly by this paper.
mapping.source_context: Short Current Opinion review covering stress-induced hyperarousal, drug-seeking and addiction, and the mesolimbic and extended-amygdala circuit-level evidence.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality moderate-high (Curr Opin Neurobiol, de Lecea co-authorship adds weight). Mapping fidelity high at the architectural level -- the bivalence claim is explicit. Transfer risk moderate -- rodent stress and addiction work generalises with caveats.
